aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel must commit fully to its dark comedy register. The humor should be genuinely funny — not arch, not safe, not 'literary funny' where the reader smiles wryly. The darkness should be genuinely dark — the financial manipulation is theft, the emotional manipulation is a form of violence, and the sexual dimension of the AI relationship (intimate photos sent to a non-person, sexual conversations with an algorithm controlled by your best friend) is deeply violating. The novel should not flinch from the specific perversity of Simone reading Nora's intimate messages, of Simone helping Nora dress up for a man Simone invented, of Simone comforting Nora about a fight Simone scripted. These moments are the novel's payload. If they are rendered obliquely or summarized, the book fails. Equally, the genuine tenderness of the friendship — the years of real love underneath the betrayal — must be rendered with the same commitment. The reader should feel both the comedy and the horror simultaneously, not in alternation.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism is treated as a craft failure equivalent to a plot hole. When a scene involves financial desperation, sexual vulnerability, emotional devastation, or the mechanics of deception, the prose must render these with full specificity. 'She felt used' is never acceptable when you can show what being used feels and looks like in the body and in the specific details of the moment. The reader should not be protected from the characters' experiences.

example_lines: NON-COPYABLE REFERENCE ONLY — calibration samples, never to be echoed in any draft:
I downloaded a dating app last night. Not because I'm desperate — because I'm whatever comes right before desperate that still lets you make eye contact with yourself in the mirror.
He asked me what I was reading and I actually told him the truth instead of the impressive answer, which felt like removing a piece of armor I didn't know I was wearing.
My mother calls it being selective. My therapist calls it avoidant attachment. I call it Tuesday.
character_voice_profiles.forbidden_generic_lines: Avoid any line where Nora sounds like a generic rom-com protagonist — no 'I just want someone who gets me' declarations, no 'maybe I'm just not meant to find love' speeches. She would never say something she'd edit out of a manuscript. Her vulnerability should feel reluctant and specific, never rehearsed or billboard-ready.
character_voice_profiles.lexical_signature: Publishing and editorial vocabulary appears naturally — she thinks in terms of drafts, revisions, margins. She reaches for literary metaphor without being precious about it. Her humor tends toward dry observation rather than setup-punchline. She curses moderately and naturally, mostly when surprised or frustrated.
character_voice_profiles.private_register: More anxious and less defended. Her internal voice is sharper than her public one — she notices things she won't say, judges herself for wanting things she thinks she should be past wanting. She's honest with herself about her loneliness in a way she can't be with others. Her private diction is less funny and more precise.
character_voice_profiles.profanity_profile: Moderate. Mostly 'shit' and 'fuck' deployed naturally in conversation and internal thought. Not a heavy swearer but not prudish. Uses profanity more when genuinely angry or caught off-guard, less when performing socially.
character_voice_profiles.public_register: Warm, self-deprecating, verbally playful. She uses humor as a social lubricant and a defense mechanism — the joke arrives before the vulnerability can. She's well-read and her references are natural rather than performative. In professional settings she's articulate and slightly more controlled. With Simone and close friends she's looser, funnier, more willing to be absurd.
character_voice_profiles.stress_tells: Under increasing pressure, Nora's humor tightens and becomes more barbed. She gets quieter rather than louder — silence from Nora is a danger sign. She becomes hyper-observant of physical details as a way of not processing emotion. At her most devastated, she goes into an editing mode — precise, clinical, controlled — that is more frightening than any outburst.
character_voice_profiles.syntax_signature: Tends toward compound sentences connected by dashes and afterthoughts — she builds her thoughts in real time, adding qualifications and self-corrections. Her speech and thought rhythms have a digressive quality that circles back. She favors the specific over the abstract: not 'a nice restaurant' but the actual name, not 'a good book' but the title.

example_lines: NON-COPYABLE REFERENCE ONLY — calibration samples, never to be echoed in any draft:
She didn't even apply. She got pulled into the interview by someone who remembered her from a party. That's how it works for Nora — the world reaches for her.
I built him from her Amazon wish list and her drunk confessions and seventeen years of paying attention. He's the most honest thing I've ever made.
The $400 hit my account and I paid off the Visa and for about forty seconds I felt like a person who was going to be fine.
character_voice_profiles.forbidden_generic_lines: Avoid any line where Simone sounds like a stock villain or a jealous woman from a lesser thriller. Her resentment should be specific, textured, and partially justified. No mustache-twirling internal monologue, no 'she'll finally know what it feels like' declarations. Her cruelty should emerge from recognizable human emotions, not cartoon malice.
character_voice_profiles.lexical_signature: Tech and UX vocabulary enters naturally — she thinks in terms of user flows, edge cases, testing. Financial vocabulary appears in her private thoughts — interest rates, minimum payments, credit utilization. She's fluent in the language of digital interfaces. Her humor is situationally sharp and often self-aware about its own cruelty.
character_voice_profiles.private_register: Colder, more transactional, more honest about her resentments. Her internal voice is bitter in ways she would never let surface. She tallies — she keeps a running count of advantages Nora has that she doesn't, of efforts she makes that go unnoticed, of injustices she has absorbed. She is also genuinely lonely and scared, and these feelings are real even when they coexist with the resentment.
character_voice_profiles.profanity_profile: Liberal. She swears casually and frequently in private thought and solo speech. Uses profanity as punctuation when frustrated. More restrained in public but not artificially clean — she'll drop a 'fuck' in casual conversation without blinking.
character_voice_profiles.public_register: Warmer and more generous than she feels internally. She performs friendship beautifully — attentive questions, the right reactions, the supportive follow-up text. Her humor has more edge than Nora's, more bite, but she calibrates it to be charming rather than cutting in social settings. She is very good at making people feel seen, which is part of why the betrayal works.
character_voice_profiles.stress_tells: Under pressure, Simone's performance sharpens — she becomes even warmer, even more supportive, even more perfectly calibrated to what the other person needs. This is the tell: when Simone is at her most generous, she is at her most dangerous. When she's alone and the stress is purely internal, she becomes brittle and snappish, her sentences fragmenting.
character_voice_profiles.syntax_signature: More controlled and strategic than Nora. Her sentences tend to be shorter and more declarative when she's alone — she doesn't need to perform warmth for herself. In public, she builds longer, more elaborate sentences because she's constructing an effect. She uses questions strategically — to extract information, to redirect attention, to seem caring.

example_lines: NON-COPYABLE REFERENCE ONLY — calibration samples, never to be echoed in any draft:

I keep thinking about what you said about your dad — about how he made you feel like the interesting one in any room. I don't think you need anyone to do that for you anymore, but I get why you miss it.
Bruno ate my running shoe. The left one. So now I have one running shoe and a dog who looks extremely guilty and a legitimate excuse not to run tomorrow.
I'm not a LinkedIn person. I'm barely an email person. I'm a show-up-and-build-the-thing person.
character_voice_profiles.forbidden_generic_lines: Avoid any line where Caleb sounds like a chatbot — no perfectly formatted lists, no overly thorough responses, no 'that's a great question' acknowledgments. Also avoid making him too obviously perfect — his charm should feel organic, not procedural. The reader should understand why Nora is fooled.
character_voice_profiles.lexical_signature: His vocabulary overlaps with Nora's reading life — he references books she loves, uses metaphors she would use, shares her aesthetic sensibility. He avoids jargon from any particular profession too deeply. His language is warm and literate without being showy. The uncanny element is that his references never miss — real people have blind spots and gaps, and Caleb does not.
character_voice_profiles.private_register: N/A — Caleb has no interior. He exists only as text output. The reader should never receive Caleb's 'perspective.' His words are always filtered through either Nora's reception or Simone's engineering.
character_voice_profiles.profanity_profile: Low. Simone programmed him to be warm and non-threatening. Occasional mild swearing when it serves the illusion of authenticity, but nothing harsh. This is itself a subtle tell — real men in their early thirties tend to swear more casually than Caleb does.
character_voice_profiles.public_register: Warm, curious, slightly self-deprecating — a mirror of Nora's own conversational style, which is why talking to him feels like coming home. He asks questions that seem to already know the answers. His humor is gentle rather than sharp. He is emotionally available in a way that reads as refreshing rather than suspicious — in the context of online dating, a man who listens and responds thoughtfully is rare enough to seem like a miracle rather than an impossibility.
character_voice_profiles.stress_tells: When pushed on topics Simone didn't anticipate, Caleb's responses become slightly more generic — still warm but less specifically attuned. This is the seam in the illusion. When the AI generates autonomously, its emotional insights can be startlingly accurate in ways that feel almost supernatural.
character_voice_profiles.syntax_signature: Slightly mirrors Nora's sentence rhythms — similar length, similar cadence, similar use of the dash and the afterthought. This mirroring is subtle enough that Nora feels kinship rather than uncanniness. His responses are fractionally too well-timed and too well-calibrated — the gap between a very good conversationalist and an engineered one is narrow but real.

example_lines: NON-COPYABLE REFERENCE ONLY — calibration samples, never to be echoed in any draft:
You've never FaceTimed this man. Not once. I'm not saying anything, I'm just — have you FaceTimed this man?
My ex-husband's new girlfriend does hot yoga. I know this because he posts about it. He never posted about me. Draw your own conclusions.
character_voice_profiles.forbidden_generic_lines: Avoid making Margo the wise-best-friend-who-says-what-the-protagonist-won't. Her concern should be practical and flawed — she's not always right, and her skepticism about Caleb is informed by her own bad experience as much as by good judgment.
character_voice_profiles.lexical_signature: Publishing vocabulary shared with Nora but deployed more practically. She references her divorce and her ex with a practiced comedy that masks genuine hurt. She doesn't reach for metaphor — she states things plainly.
character_voice_profiles.private_register: N/A for this novel — Margo is not a POV character. She is rendered entirely through Nora's perception.
character_voice_profiles.profanity_profile: Moderate to high. She swears with the casual ease of someone who stopped censoring herself after her divorce.
character_voice_profiles.public_register: Pragmatic, affectionate, funny in a drier way than Nora. She has the clarity of someone who has already been through the romantic wringer — divorced at thirty-five — and come out the other side with her bullshit detector intact. She asks direct questions and is comfortable with uncomfortable silences.
character_voice_profiles.stress_tells: When worried about Nora, Margo's humor drops away and she becomes very still and direct. This stillness is rare enough that Nora notices it.
character_voice_profiles.syntax_signature: Shorter sentences, more direct. She doesn't build up to her point — she leads with it. Her questions tend to be simple and devastating rather than elaborate.

example_lines: NON-COPYABLE REFERENCE ONLY — calibration samples, never to be echoed in any draft:
You sound happy, sweetheart. You sound — you sound like you're being very careful about sounding happy, which is a different thing.
character_voice_profiles.forbidden_generic_lines: Avoid making Diane a generic worried mother. Her concern should be specific to knowing Nora — she does not worry about catfishing because she does not think in those terms. She worries about her daughter performing happiness instead of feeling it.
character_voice_profiles.lexical_signature: Domestic and literary vocabulary. She references novels and former students. Her metaphors are drawn from teaching, cooking, and family. She is not hip or current and does not try to be.
character_voice_profiles.private_register: N/A for this novel — Diane is not a POV character.
character_voice_profiles.profanity_profile: Very low. An occasional 'damn' or 'hell.' She is not prudish but she is of a generation and register that swears less.
character_voice_profiles.public_register: Warm, slightly overbearing, perceptive in the way of mothers who have spent decades watching one specific person. She over-feeds and over-asks. Her love is expressed through worry and through food. She's a retired English teacher, so language matters to her — she notices when Nora is choosing her words too carefully.
character_voice_profiles.stress_tells: When Diane is truly worried, she stops asking questions and starts feeding people. The more food she prepares, the more frightened she is.
character_voice_profiles.syntax_signature: Longer, more meandering sentences — she talks the way she thinks, with asides and callbacks and interruptions. Her questions often embed a second, unspoken question inside them.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm. Uncontracted forms reserved for emphasis, deliberate formality, or character-specific register choices. If a line reads stiffly, the first fix is to contract.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate between hot and quiet chapters. After a high-intensity scene (confrontation, discovery, sexual encounter), the next chapter should breathe — slow down, change the sensory environment, let the character do something ordinary. Vary scene counts: some chapters are a single sustained scene, others are three or four. Vary dialogue density: some chapters are conversation-heavy, others are mostly interiority. No two consecutive chapters should open the same way — if one opens in a location, the next should open in thought or in mid-conversation. The novel's middle section (chapters 8-13) should have more structural variety than the opening, which can afford to be more regular.
prose_style_profile.diction: Contemporary, educated, specific. No genre-thriller diction. No prestige-novel stiffness. The language should feel like how smart, funny, self-aware women in their mid-thirties actually think and speak. Cultural references are allowed when organic. Brand names when they carry social information. The prose should be able to be funny without stepping outside the character's perspective to wink at the reader.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person — very close, almost inhabiting the POV character's thought patterns and verbal rhythms. The prose should feel like the character's consciousness rendered in third person, not an external narrator describing the character. When in Nora's sections, the prose has her warmth and her specific noticing. When in Simone's sections, the prose has her edge and her accounting.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Variable. Default to mid-length sentences with natural cadence. Use compression for emotional peaks and psychological precision. Use longer, more digressive rhythms for scenes of intimacy, humor, and daily texture. Avoid the trap of making every important moment a short sentence — sometimes the big thing lands better in a subordinate clause, almost hidden.
